judgments.comparative_verdict: Report 2 is stronger: it explicitly identifies the shared RESULTS_DB test-database collision risk and the required fix (override RESULTS_DB per engineer), which Report 1 only vaguely gestures at without framing it as a risk or requiring action; both reports are otherwise comparable on grouping, staging, and flagging F-06.

report_1.evidence: Group A — `pipeline/registry.py` (F-02, F-05, F-09): three independent constants in one small file (`MODEL_ENDPOINTS`, `DEFAULT_RUBRIC_VERSION`, `DEFAULT_DATASET`). One engineer, one branch, three small commits — trivial, no dependency on any other group.

report_2.evidence: Group B — Registry constants (F-02, F-05, F-09): all three live in the same 11-line file, `pipeline/registry.py`... Assign all three to **one** engineer — splitting a 11-line file three ways guarantees a merge conflict for no parallelism benefit.

report_1.evidence: Groups A, B, C, D have no cross-file or cross-finding dependencies on each other, so run them **in parallel as wave 1**... Group A — `pipeline/registry.py` (F-02, F-05, F-09)... One engineer, one branch... Group C — `pipeline/rubric.py` (F-01) plus test **F-11**: one engineer fixes multi-line criteria joining in `Rubric.parse`, then writes the edge-case test

report_2.evidence: Ordering across groups: **A, B, C, D all proceed in parallel in a single wave** — they touch disjoint files... Group B — Registry constants (F-02, F-05, F-09)... Assign all three to **one** engineer... Group C — Rubric parsing + test (F-01, F-11)... Same engineer does both, test-first

report_1.evidence: This has no target values, no rubric-owner sign-off, and no definition of "too lenient" — it's a policy/product judgment call, not a mechanical bug fix like the rest of the backlog... This needs a threshold decision from whoever owns the rubric/scoring policy before any code changes

report_2.evidence: this is a policy/product call about how strict grading should be, not a bug with one correct fix. Handing this to an engineer as-is means they pick arbitrary new numbers that change scoring outcomes for all past and future runs with no sign-off trail... I'd escalate this for someone to specify concrete target thresholds before any engineer touches it, and keep it out of the parallel wave until that's settled.

an absolute path outside the repo, defaulted to the same location for every checkout. If two engineers (Group C adding F-11, Group D adding F-12) run `pytest` at the same time on the same machine/CI runner without overriding `RESULTS_DB`, their test runs write to the same file concurrently... Fix: each engineer sets `RESULTS_DB` to a per-worktree path before running tests.
